speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Gerald Grossman. Interviewed by Bonnie Dwork. And videotaped by Ed [? Stabler ?] in Bayside, New York, USA. Just a moment. I'll tell you when. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was in Feldafing. And as I said, they had to take me to a hospital. In order to-- to be there, my-- they-- I was given a name by Leon [? Sirling ?] from the documents, one of the guys that already left for Israel. And as Leon [? Sirling, ?] I continued over there in a hospital for two weeks. Was treated by a doctor, a Nazi. He used to experiment in the concentration camps. He was given five years for that. And the-- the Jewish organization needed a doctor. So they took him out of jail so he could work for the Jews. This is the Nazi that treated me for my injuries. After that, I was sent back to Stuttgart. I lived there in a camp-- it was sort of scattered-- for a short time. And I received a letter from Austria that the papers are coming through for me to go back-- to go to Canada. And I traveled from Stuttgart back to Linz [INAUDIBLE]. And over there, we made all our papers. The papers came through from the UNRRA from the-- for the displaced persons. And I was taken in a group of tailors to Canada, where I arrived in Halifax March the 8th of 1948. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From Halifax, we traveled for two days in a train to Montreal. I was placed in a private house by the HIAS to live on-- on one of the streets, Clark Street, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where I was working as a tailor in factories in Montreal for a while. My family from the United States came to visit me. For the first time I met them. I never knew them before. And I also met my wife, which lives across the street from where I live. And we got married in 1950. And in 1951, my papers came through, and I came to the United States, to New York, by train. I was greeted by my family here in the United States. And life became different.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had to establish myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Started to-- to work and make a living for my family. I had my first daughter was born in 1953. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went into business with a partner in 1953, and we opened up a service station, being that I was a mechanic before and my partner was also a mechanic. He was-- he survived the concentration camp. He used to work for the German government during the war. And together, we were 35 years in business as in automotive business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you retired when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired in 1988.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what have you done since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since then, I devoting my time for Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am working for the Red Cross in a specialty-- is Project Search. We are looking for relatives for the Holocaust survivors of the Second World War. And we are finding mostly dead. But I'm working there for the last three and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find out about your parents and your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1988, my wife, my daughter, and I made a trip to the city of Rovno. And we met a lot of people that lived down there. I'm still searching for them. I never gave up the-- the idea that they were dead. But what I found out is that in the city of Rovno, there were a couple of massacres. The first massacre was right away when the Germans came into the city and they wanted to clear the city of the Jews. They needed the area because the headquarters of the German Gestapo was in Rovno, not in the city of Kiev. About seven generals were stationed in the city of Rovno. The first of the massacres that occurred was on November the 6th and the 7th of 1941, when they brought out 17,500 Jews to the woods of the Sosenski. That's what they're called. We used to go there at the same woods [INAUDIBLE]. It's a Jewish holiday where we used to celebrate and go there to these woods. That's the woods they-- they decided to take the Jews, where the Russian prisoners and the Ukrainian people were digging the graves. They brought the 17,500 Jews to these graves. And within two days, the Germans and the Ukrainian militia that they had were shooting the people. I know of only two survivors that were-- that are still alive today from those graves. They crawled out in the middle of the night, and Polish people and Czechoslovakian people saved them. I spoke to them. And no survivors of that. Was left in Rovno until 1942. Sometimes in May of 1942, there was still a ghetto, where 5,000 Jews were left. The 5,000 Jews were taken to Kostopol, a town not far from Rovno. And they were shot there. And practically, most of the Jews from the city of Rovno were killed. The rest of the year, whatever survived after the war, I have a picture of survivors of the Rovno, of a whole group that survived. Some live in the United States, Canada, Israel, Argentina. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I am now the president of the Rovno Society here in the city of New York. We have mostly survivors, about 170 members. I am already president for the last five years. And we try to-- to get in touch with each other every once in a while. We have meetings, about six meetings a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your thoughts about that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody could believe that something like that would happen. And it's very hard to believe in today's days, about concentration camps, about refugees to-- to Russia, about the destruction of the Jewish people in Europe. It wouldn't take long to-- to have them all killed if it wasn't for the Russian government fighting with Germany, the United States, England, Canada. And I would say thanks to these governments that defeated Germany, there are some Jews left. And naturally, all of those Jews, most of the refugees that came back to Poland, that had a population of 3 and 1/2 million, I supposed today there are only about 5,000 Jews living in Poland. And most of them probably assimilated, and the rest of them went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to thank you very much for telling us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, how are we doing with time?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're going to introduce your wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: As soon as--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Florence. We met in Montreal just about a few years after I lived there. And she can probably tell you the rest of our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm Florence Williams Grossman. We're married 45 years. And we were married in 1950, September the 3rd. We have two daughters and four grandchildren. It's quite an accomplishment. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Very good. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Enough?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is a picture I found here in the United States. My mother and father when they got married. My father was David. My mother Tsipa. And they got married in 1922. OK. This is a picture I also found over here after the war. When I came to the United States, one of my aunts had it. It was sent to them in 1935. And my father is on top. The-- the tallest one standing here, that's me. From left to right, there was my sister Feiga, my brother Pesach. My mother is holding the youngest child, Motel. And to the right is Isar, my second brother. This is a picture that I found over here also from my uncle, the one that found me in Russia and in Linz. That was his son-- Geshe was his name-- before the war. His son, his wife, and his mother-in-law, and also a sister-in-law got killed the first day when they bombarded Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a picture of me that I found in the United States. A copy of this picture I had in Russia with me, which helped me to get out and get documents to go to Poland. This picture is got a uniform from a Zionist organization that I belonged to before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only picture I have from Russia where we were in Donskoy. That is the last place that I was in. And on this picture are the only survivors from Donskoy and that left for Poland. The first one, from left to right, is [? Steinburg, ?] [? Jacobovich, ?] [? Yablonski, ?] [? Bich. ?] This-- the one in the middle, sitting [INAUDIBLE] is [? Roysman. ?] He was the-- the chief of our group, a Russian. Did not go back to Poland. The lady is [? Pithcari. ?] She was the secretary, also from Poland. I don't know the-- the next two. But one of-- one of them is [? Berlich, ?] on the left, all the way on the bottom. And I am on the right side on the bottom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a second. And three, two, one. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the city of Rovno. Sort of located on the-- in Ukraine. I-- I-- that's where I grew up. I went to school to Zdolbuniv, which is right next to Rovno over here. From Zdolbuniv, I traveled to work in [INAUDIBLE], over here, which is right on the border. In 1941 is the Germans were on this side, and the Russians were on this side. And I was working right over here on the border near Lvov. From there, I traveled back to Zdolbuniv. And in Zdolbuniv, I had to go through Zhytomyr, Poltava, Kharkov, and to Moscow. This is where the Moscow-- a railroad was built all the way around Moscow. From Moscow, I travel to Kalinin, Kalinin to Gorky, Kuybyshev, and all the way to Tashkent. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So Gorky, Kuybyshev, and then to Tashkent. Tashkent to Guzar to Sikorski army. And then back to [INAUDIBLE] to the [INAUDIBLE] over here and the [INAUDIBLE] on this side. From there, back to [INAUDIBLE] and to Akhtubinsk There, we went to Donskoy, which was the last place I was in. When 1946, back to Akhtubinsk and from Akhtubinsk traveled back through the Caspian Sea, along over here on this side, and into Kharkov, Poltava, Kiev, past by Rovno into Krakow of Poland, and settle in [? Bittem ?] [INAUDIBLE]. I don't think is the right place for it, but it's in [? Bittem. ?] From [? Bittem ?] to Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia to-- to Austria, Germany, Stuttgart, and back to Linz. From Linz to Bremerhaven and to Canada, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many minutes have we got left?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, sir.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me joined the Jewish police in the camp-- DP camp of Bindermichl in Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my family taken in 1994. From left to right, at top is Bob Fenster, my son-in-law, Mike Kennedy, a second son-in-law. I am standing on the right. Then comes my daughter Isabelle Fenster, Harriet Kennedy, my wife. The bottom row is my oldest grandson, David Fenster. Then comes Mitchel Kennedy and Danielle Fenster.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side is my youngest one, Eric Kennedy. And to the right is his brother Mitchel Kennedy, which is now six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my only granddaughter that I have. And that's Danielle Fenster. And she is 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest grandson. He has now past 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is David Fenster.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from Rovno recently taken by a friend of ours. And that shows the monument where the 17,500 Jews were killed in this area.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Rovno in 1988, we went to visit the spot where they killed all the Jews. And I found the bones and all kind of clothing right in this area. I picked up some earth and brought it back to the United States, where we made a monument for a memorial for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This book is called written by Douglas K. Huneke about Fritz Graebe, which was a director of the Zdolbuniv Station. Under his supervision, he had-- he tried to save a lot of Jews. He is a witness, tried to get out the Jews while they were being shot. He testified in Nuremberg. And he is a righteous Christian. And his tree is in Jerusalem. And his plaque is in Jerusalem right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] I see your grandchildren are the ones that count. I'll cue this up myself [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you again very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: I feel privileged to have listened to your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I understand I'm going to get some information?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I'm going to have them send a copy of that release. And in two months, you'll get the tape. OK? And I'll give you a call in a couple of days to see how you feel. [INAUDIBLE].
